videos.title: Impossible Gears
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith demonstrates a series of 3D-printed mechanisms he calls "impossible gears" — gear arrangements that produce a non-1:1 gear reduction ratio even when all gears involved are exactly the same size, contradicting a long-held assumption in mechanical engineering. He builds and tests five distinct mechanisms across the video (a week-long series), achieving ratios of 2:1 and 3:1 from same-sized gears, and closes with a mathematical explanation rooted in epicycloids and epicyclic geometry.

## Key Topics
- Gear ratio fundamentals: pitch circle, tooth count as shorthand,...
key_topics: Gear ratio fundamentals: pitch circle, tooth count as shorthand, drive vs. driven relationships
The "impossible" premise: why same-sized gears were assumed to always yield a 1:1 ratio
Mechanism 1: Spur gears with a lever/bar arm producing a 2:1 ratio and simultaneous power split across two axles
Mechanism 2: Three identical bevel gears in a fixed/rotating arrangement producing a 2:1 ratio
Mechanism 3: Three bevel gears with an additional modular stage producing a 3:1 ratio (and back-drivability)
Mechanism 4: Same-sized spur gears in a rotating cage with a fixed gear producing a 2:1 ratio
Mechanism 5: Trammel of Archimedes (ellipsograph / "do nothing machine") incorporated into a gear mechanism for a 2:1 ratio
Mathematical explanation: epicycloids, rolling circles, and why the path length constraint is satisfied
Connection to planetary gear systems and the general formula for epicycloid lobes: **n + 1** (where n = ratio of fixed to rolling circle radii)
Leibniz's philosophical concept of the monad as an aside on infinitesimal points
materials_mentioned: 3D-printed PLA parts (designed in Tinkercad, files released on Thingiverse)
Small clips and pegs (press-fit fasteners for assembly)
Adhesive/glue (used to fix certain gears rigidly to their axles)
Silicone oil (applied to the Trammel of Archimedes mechanism to reduce friction and allow smooth operation)

videos.title: Putting In A Simple Solar System - Omnibus
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith installs a small off-grid solar power system to run festoon lights and a resin 3D printer (Halot X1) in his garden shed and pergola area. The video doubles as a practical tutorial covering solar system design from energy audit through to wiring and distribution, with Robert advocating for a 12V DC-first approach to avoid the inefficiency of converting solar DC → AC → DC.

## Key Topics
- Energy audit: calculating load requirements before sizing a solar system
- Panel selection and optimal mounting angle (35° south-facing for southern England latitude)
- Solar ch...
key_topics: Energy audit: calculating load requirements before sizing a solar system
Panel selection and optimal mounting angle (35° south-facing for southern England latitude)
Solar charge controller types: PWM vs MPPT — trade-offs between cost and efficiency
Redundant system design philosophy (inspired by RAID arrays)
Battery type comparison: flooded lead acid, gel, AGM, lithium-ion variants, and exotic chemistries
Series vs parallel wiring of panels and batteries
12V DC distribution as an alternative to inverter-based AC systems
Voltage drop considerations for low-voltage DC systems
Practical wiring: cable sizing, lugs, fuse boards, isolators, and bus bars
DC-to-DC buck/boost converters as cheap voltage adapters
Drip loop technique for outdoor cable entry
materials_mentioned: **120W monocrystalline solar panels** (×4, 670×960×35mm) — main generation source
**Lead acid batteries** (flooded FLA type, ×4, ~4 kWh total) — energy storage; noted for hydrogen off-gassing risk requiring ventilation
**Gel lead acid batteries** — mentioned as a lead battery variant
**AGM (Absorbent Glass Mat) batteries** — noted for better longevity than flooded FLA
**Lithium manganese dioxide** — exotic lithium chemistry with tens-of-thousands of charge cycles
**Lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4)** — popular lithium variant, hundreds of charge cycles
**Nickel-iron (NiFe) batteries** — old but long-lived exotic option, still manufactured in China
**Zinc bromide gravity battery** — home-buildable exotic chemistry Robert wants to experiment with
**Hammerite paint** — single-coat paint used on welded steel panel brackets

**Heat shrink / electrician's tape** — cable insulation finishing
**Hot glue** — used to prevent internal contact inside project box

videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith presents an omnibus episode covering the development of his "vortex" cylindrical wind turbine — a ring-shaped rotor with an Archimedes screw interior and integrated generator. He demonstrates how to measure static torque to evaluate wind capture device performance, shows that adding fins to a flying-gyroscope-style rotor produces roughly a fivefold increase in torque, and walks through the full electronics chain from AC coil output through rectification and capacitor smoothing to usable DC. The episode concludes with TinkerCad tutorials for producing cube and he...
key_topics: Measuring static torque to evaluate wind turbine rotor designs without a complete generator
The flying gyroscope as a wind turbine concept (ring-wing / annular airfoil)
Effect of external vs. internal airfoil bumps on torque output
Effect of adding fins (internally and externally) to the cylindrical rotor
Archimedes screw interior fins integrated into the cylindrical rotor
Integrated generator design: neodymium magnets embedded in the rotor cylinder, serpentine coil in the stator
Coil winding theory: turns, wire gauge, voltage, current, and power relationships
Custom coil-winding machine for hair-thin wire
AC-to-DC conversion using a bridge rectifier and electrolytic capacitor
TinkerCad workflow for designing cube and hexagonal wind-wall housings
Weatherproofing with potting compound or paraffin wax
"Wind Cube" and "Wind Tube" as stackable modular turbine concepts
materials_mentioned: **Neodymium magnets** (1 cm × 3 mm) — embedded in the rotor cylinder in a north-south alternating pattern to form the rotor of the integrated generator
**Superglue** — used to fix magnets in place
**32 SWG (Standard Wire Gauge) copper wire** — hair-thin magnet wire used for the 1,000-turn coil
**Electrician's tape** — used to bind and hold the wound serpentine coil together
**Electrolytic capacitor** (1,000 µF / 50 V) — used to smooth rectified DC output
**Bridge rectifier module** (2 A / 1,000 V) — bought in bulk from eBay, used to convert AC to DC
**Paraffin wax / potting compound** — mentioned for weatherproofing the electronics cavity
**PLA or similar 3D-print filament** — implied throughout for all printed parts (not stated explicitly)
**8 mm skate bearings** — used as radial and thrust bearings in the rotor cradle

videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
This episode of "Man In A Shed" is a wide-ranging survey of alternative vehicle propulsion technologies, covering the rise and fall of electric cars, hydrogen generation from aluminium and water, ammonia as a fuel, zinc-chlorine flow batteries, wind-powered cars, and the long history of "water car" claims. Robert argues that the death and rebirth of the electric car was driven purely by consumer interest rather than conspiracy, and he highlights several underexplored technologies — particularly the aluminium-water hydrogen system and Graneau's cavitation research — as genuinely pro...
key_topics: History of electric vehicles from 1828 (Robert Anderson) through the Edison-Ford project to the modern EV revival
Why EVs died (battery limitations, cheap oil, cheaper petrol cars) and why they returned (consumer demand, government policy)
Current EV shortcomings: cost, charging infrastructure, insurance, energy source still largely fossil fuels
Hydrogen generation via aluminium + potassium hydroxide (the Yeno method), including live demonstration
The Francois Cornish patent (US 4,702,894) for an aluminium-wire/water hydrogen generator, and BMW's trials
Ammonia as a combustion fuel: history (X-15 rocket plane, Belgian WWII buses), GAC/Toyota production ammonia car, infrastructure advantages
Zinc-chlorine flow batteries: Gulf Western's 1970s system, Stanford's 2021 microporous carbon advance, 2023 manganese dioxide cathode paper, and the NanoFlowcell production car
Wind-powered vehicles: Greenbird, Blackbird, Savonius rotor model build, Dutch inventor Oscar DEA's turbine-charged EV
The "water car" — clarifying what it is and is not, history of fraud cases, and Graneau's cavitation-based approach as the only legitimate candidate
materials_mentioned: **Potassium hydroxide (KOH), 5 M concentrated solution** — electrolyte/reactant in the Yeno method hydrogen generation demo
**Aluminium (foil and wire)** — reacts with KOH to produce hydrogen; also used as the metal in Cornish's patented system
**Potassium aluminate** — reaction product of aluminium + KOH
**Hydrogen (H₂)** — gas produced by Yeno method; fuel for hydrogen cars and the Cornish BMW trial
**Nickel-iron battery (Edison's "nickeline" battery)** — intended for Ford-Edison EV project; had high internal resistance issues
**Lead-acid battery** — the limiting battery technology of early EVs
**Ammonia (NH₃)** — zero-CO₂ combustion fuel (burns to N₂ + H₂O); used in X-15 aircraft, Belgian WWII buses, University of Michigan cross-country drive, and GAC/Toyota 2 L saloon car
**Liquid oxygen / hydrogen (promoters)** — added at ~3% to make ammonia burn as fast as conventional fuel
**Zinc chloride (ZnCl₂)** — electrolyte in zinc-chlorine flow battery
**Chlorine (Cl₂)** — cathode reactant in zinc-chlorine battery; stored as chlorine hydrate
**Chlorine hydrate (Cl₂·8H₂O)** — ice-like frozen storage form of chlorine in Gulf Western battery
**Microporous / activated carbon** — used by Stanford (2021) to trap chlorine (and other halogens) in battery cathode
**Manganese dioxide (MnO₂)** — cathode coating in 2023 paper that improved zinc-chlorine battery reaction kinetics
**Sodium / lithium** — metals substituted for zinc in Stanford's updated chlorine battery work
**Sodium chloride (saltwater)** — claimed working fluid of NanoFlowcell's "Quantino" flow-battery car
**HHO / Brown's gas** — partial electrolysis product, clarified as *not* the basis of a true "water car"
**Metal hydride** — mentioned in context of the 2008 Japanese Genipax claim

videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
"Man In A Shed - Episode 8" is a wide-ranging episode in which Robert covers six distinct science and engineering topics, each centred on energy generation or conversion. The through-line is his enthusiasm for open-access research and counter-intuitive engineering solutions — from soil-powered sensors to nuclear fusion milestones. His central takeaway is that simpler approaches (vertical panels, Fibonacci blade shapes, switched reluctance) often outperform complex ones.

## Key Topics
- **Microbial fuel cells** — how they work, and Northwestern McCormick School's new perpendicular ...
key_topics: **Microbial fuel cells** — how they work, and Northwestern McCormick School's new perpendicular 3D-printed design that outperforms competing designs by 120%
**Fluorescent acrylic and 3D-printed fluorescent filament** as light guides/concentrators for solar cells
**Concentrated solar photovoltaics (CSPv)** — HyperStealth's lenticular lens + mirror combination for boosting solar panel output
**Vertical bifacial solar panels** — Dutch research showing temperature-driven efficiency gains from hanging panels vertically
**Nuclear fusion breakthrough** — NIF's inertial confinement fusion experiment (confirming 2022 results) achieving net energy gain (~2× energy out vs. in)
**Switched reluctance / flux-switching alternators** — the Alexanderson generator principle, guitar pickups, and missile power supplies
**Spiral wind turbines** — Fibonacci/conical spiral Savonius-type blades, including the Liam F1 commercial turbine
materials_mentioned: **Carbon felt** — used as electrodes in the Northwestern microbial fuel cell (cheap, abundant, inert)
**Water-jetted stainless steel** — used for some parts of the Northwestern MFC prototype (noted as reproducible by hand with a hacksaw)
**Fluorescent acrylic sheet** — acts as a luminescent solar concentrator / light guide
**Fluorescent yellow PLA filament** (from 123-3D) — found to be transparent and act as an optical fibre / light guide when 3D printed
**Lenticular / prism film** (salvaged from an LCD screen) — used to concentrate light onto a solar cell
**Aluminium foil** — tested as a mirror substitute in the CSPv experiment
**Deuterium and tritium** — hydrogen isotopes used as fusion fuel at NIF (220 micrograms per capsule)
**High-density carbon capsule** (4.25 mg) — the fuel pellet housing in the NIF experiment
**Steel / iron** — used in the switched reluctance demonstration to redirect magnetic flux

videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert surveys a range of innovative vertical axis and alternative wind turbine designs, covering the O-Wind tetrahedron turbine, the Harmony furling Savonius turbine, crossflow turbines (including the Ridge Blade), Fibonacci spiral turbines, kite-based airborne wind generation, and triboelectric nanogenerators (TENG) for wind energy. He 3D-prints and bench-tests several of these designs himself. His central conclusion is that no single wind energy technology suits all environments or users — diversity of approach, matched to local conditions and resources, is the real path forward...
key_topics: O-Wind turbine: James Dyson Award-winning tetrahedral VAWT designed to capture wind from any direction
O-Wind patent analysis (US Patent 2021/0349530 A1) and 3D-printed model demonstration
Savonius turbine basics: differential drag, efficiency (~15% historically, ~30% per recent research)
Harmony wind turbine: furling Savonius design by Christopher Mo, allowing variable blade pitch to operate across a wider wind speed range
Crossflow wind turbines: history (Paul Mortier 1891, Banki-Mitchell, Ossberger), design parameters, advantages and limitations
Ridge Blade: a commercial rooftop crossflow turbine
Fibonacci/conical spiral turbines: research results showing up to 73.1% power coefficient; inspiration for the Liam F1 turbine
Types of VAWT: Darrieus and Savonius subtypes
Types of spirals: Fibonacci, logarithmic, golden ratio, Archimedes
Kite-based airborne wind energy: history of kites, two generation modes (drum/rope lift-cycle and rotating tether), scalability, Sky Sails Power example
Triboelectric nanogenerators (TENG) for wind energy: flutter/flapping and rotating-disc approaches
FLAG project (Flex Linear Aerostatic Generator): Canadian triboelectric flag generator
Philosophical argument for energy diversity over single-solution optimisation
materials_mentioned: **Steel bar (8 mm)** — used as the axis of rotation in the 3D-printed O-Wind model
**608 skate bearings (22 mm × 8 mm × 7 mm)** — fitted in the O-Wind model foot for rotation
**PTFE (polytetrafluoroethylene)** — used as one of the two materials in flutter-type triboelectric wind generators
**Kapton** — paired with PTFE in flexible triboelectric generators to generate high charge and current
**Aluminium** — deposited as a thin layer on plastic in triboelectric generator prototypes
**Fiberglass** — noted in the context of conventional wind tower construction and its long-term waste problem
**Concrete** — noted as a large material input for conventional wind tower foundations
**Plastic (3D-printed PLA/filament implied)** — used for all printed turbine components

## Overview
"Man In A Shed – Episode 6" is a wide-ranging episode in which Robert covers five distinct science and engineering topics: a 3D-printed dual-input planetary gearbox that acts as an electronically controlled continuously variable transmission (CVT); a critical look at the Betaray spherical solar concentrator alongside hands-on optics experiments; a smart adaptive roofing tile that uses wax motors to switch between super-black and super-white surfaces; the surprising resurgence of vertical axis wind turbines (VAWTs); the conversion of HDPE plastic into spider silk via bacterial ferme...
key_topics: Explanation of planetary gear systems: components, operating modes, and ratio calculations
3D-printed dual-input planetary gearbox with external ring-gear teeth, enabling a CVT
Self-regulating wind turbine speed control using an idler gear as a passive feedback mechanism
Betaray spherical solar concentrator (André Broessel / Rawlemon) — critical review of its claimed 57% efficiency
Hands-on experiments: bullseye car-headlight lens and fluorescent-acrylic waveguide applied to a small solar cell
Adaptive roofing tile from UC Santa Barbara: wax motor + super-black + super-white paint, claimed 2.5× reduction in heating/cooling energy
History and economics of horizontal axis wind turbines (HAWTs) — materials limits, cost per MWh, degradation rates

Destructive distillation of HDPE plastic into wax/fuel

Historical use of animals in warfare: Soviet tank dogs, American bat bombs (Project X-Ray), Project Pigeon (B.F. Skinner)
materials_mentioned: **HDPE (high-density polyethylene)** — milk bottles and plastic bags used as feedstock for destructive distillation into wax and spider silk precursor
**Strontium titanium trioxide** — catalyst used to lower the temperature required for HDPE pyrolysis

videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
"Man In A Shed - Episode 5" is a wide-ranging episode covering five distinct topics: solar energy and Prussian Blue photo-rechargeable batteries, solar desalination, electricity generation from plants, aluminium as a hydrogen fuel source, and unusual gear and power transmission mechanisms. Robert demonstrates several experiments himself — making Prussian Blue from scratch and building a working solar battery — while reviewing recent research papers across all topics. The overarching theme is simple, low-cost, and elegant engineering solutions to major energy and resource challenges...
key_topics: Solar cell efficiency history: copper oxide (~4%), silicon (18–22%), perovskites (~25%), tandem cells (~30%), and the Shockley–Queisser theoretical limit (~33.7%)
Prussian Blue as a self-recharging solar battery: bleaches white when discharging, returns to blue when exposed to sunlight
Solar water desalination: simple solar stills, evaporation enhancement using Oasis foam and black carbon felt, and MIT's thermohaline circulation method to prevent salt clogging
Three methods for extracting electricity from plants: microbial fuel cells at roots, direct photosynthesis from leaf cells, and transpiration-driven ion flow
Aluminium's reactivity: stripping its oxide layer to react with water and generate hydrogen on demand
Hydrogen-on-demand systems for vehicles: gallium–aluminium alloy method and the François Cornish patent (tested by BMW in 1981)
Gear types: spur, bevel, worm, mangle, oval, cube, heart, brain, Borromean ring, and the Venix/ABM spherical gear
Power transmission mechanisms: shaft couplings, chain/gear/belt drives, lead screws, the Coulisse (slide) mechanism, Hobson coupler, and spherical Coulisse drive
materials_mentioned: **Potassium ferricyanide (K3)** — used with iron(II) sulfate to synthesise Prussian Blue
**Potassium ferrocyanide (K4)** — alternative route to Prussian Blue, used with iron(III) chloride
**Iron(II) sulfate** — iron salt / fertiliser used in Prussian Blue synthesis
**Iron(III) chloride** — alternative iron salt for Prussian Blue synthesis
**Potassium chloride (3 M solution)** — electrolyte in the Prussian Blue battery
**Prussian Blue** (iron cyanide pigment) — photorechargeable battery active material; the basis of blueprints
**Aluminium** — reactive metal used for hydrogen generation and as electrode in Prussian Blue cell
**Potassium hydroxide / sodium hydroxide** — used to strip aluminium oxide coating and drive the aluminium–water reaction
**Gallium** — mixed with aluminium to create an alloy that continuously exposes bare aluminium to water; reclaimed and reused
**Mercury** — mentioned historically (used by Ørsted to separate aluminium; can strip oxide layer, but not recommended)
**Indium, tin, bismuth** — mentioned as candidate alloying elements for aluminium hydrogen-on-demand systems
**Nitrogen** — recent (as of 2024) additive to activate aluminium, improving hydrogen output
**Platinum and iron electrodes** — used in leaf-based living solar cells
**Activated carbon felt** — black material used to absorb solar energy and accelerate evaporation
**Oasis foam** — floral foam used to wick water to the evaporating surface
**Stainless steel mesh** — used as top electrode (light-transparent) in the Prussian Blue cell
**Supercapacitor** — used to measure and store energy output from the Prussian Blue cell

## Overview
"Man In A Shed - Episode 4" is a wide-ranging science and maker episode in which Robert covers four largely independent topics: converting coffee grounds into concrete-strengthening biochar, designing a 3D-printed wind flutter energy harvester, explaining and demonstrating nuclear (alpha-voltaic) batteries, and exploring the history of unconventional engine fuels culminating in hand-mixed gunpowder and a 3D-printed compressed air engine. The overarching theme is that inexpensive, low-tech approaches can reproduce cutting-edge or historically interesting science.

## Key Topics
- Co...
key_topics: Coffee ground biochar as a concrete additive (up to 30% strength increase)
History and chemistry of Roman pozzolanic concrete
Wind energy harvesting via flutter, vortex shedding, galloping, and wind-induced vibration
3D-printed integral-hinge flutter energy harvesting device
Betavolt BV100 nuclear battery (nickel-63 + diamond semiconductor)
History of nuclear/radioisotope batteries from 1950s pacemakers to Bristol/Russian carbon-14 diamond batteries
DIY alpha-voltaic nuclear battery demonstration using americium-241 and a transistor
Historical unconventional engine fuels: acetylene, ethanol, coal dust, liquid air, ammonia, compressed air, gunpowder
Gunpowder chemistry, manufacture, and burn-rate tuning
Charlie Macklin's gunpowder-powered oscillating steam engine
3D-printed single-acting compressed air engine (minimal-part design)
materials_mentioned: **Coffee grounds** — feedstock pyrolysed into biochar for concrete additive
**Biochar** — porous carbon-rich charcoal formed by pyrolysis; replaces 15% of sand by volume in concrete
**Activated carbon** — used as an oxygen-absorbing layer during the pyrolysis kiln process
**Pozzolan (pozzolanic ash)** — volcanic alumino-silicate used by Romans; reacts with calcium hydroxide to form insoluble hydrates
**Calcium hydroxide / lime** — reacts with pozzolan in the presence of water
**Calcium silicate hydrate / calcium aluminate hydrate** — reaction products that give Roman concrete its durability
**Nickel-63** — radioactive isotope (beta emitter) used in Betavolt BV100 nuclear battery; decays to harmless copper
**Single-crystal diamond** — semiconductor layers in the BV100 (10-micron layers sandwiching 2-micron Ni-63)
**Carbon-14** — radioactive graphite produced in nuclear reactors; used by Bristol University to grow radioactive diamond sheets for a battery
**Americium-241** — alpha-emitting radioisotope inside ionisation-type smoke alarms; used in the DIY nuclear battery demo; half-life ~432 years
**2N3055 transistor** — NPN silicon transistor used as the semiconductor junction in the DIY alpha-voltaic demo
**Potassium nitrate (saltpeter)** — oxidiser in gunpowder (75% by weight); food-grade, also sold as stump remover
**Sulfur** — fuel and ignition-temperature reducer in gunpowder (10% by weight)
**Softwood charcoal (pine)** — carbon fuel component of gunpowder (15% by weight); preferred over pure carbon
**Graphite** — tumbled onto corned gunpowder grains to prevent moisture ingress and static build-up
**Acetylene** — historically explored engine fuel; dangerous above 20 psi and forms explosive copper acetylide in brass/copper pipes
**Copper acetylide** — percussive explosive formed when acetylene contacts copper or brass
**Ethanol** — early external-combustion engine working fluid; expansion-engine variant predates internal combustion use
**Plutonium** — used in 1970s nuclear pacemaker batteries; ~88-year lifespan

videos.title: Man In A Shed - Episode 1 - A Pilot Re-uploaded
videos.description:
This was deleted in error so I have re-uploaded it
It is  compilation of videos and has videos:

videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
This pilot/omnibus episode of "Man In A Shed" is a wide-ranging tour through Robert's current obsessions, connecting ideas across nature, engineering, sustainability, and alternative energy. He draws a thread from pliosaur swimming biomechanics through to dual-rotor wind turbines, then pivots through shopping-trolley wheelchair hacks, 3D scanning cultural heritage, a carbon "forever battery" replication, and finally a gravity-powered generator. The throughline is that nature and overlooked junk are both packed with engineering solutions waiting to be noticed.

## Key Topics
- Plios...
key_topics: Pliosaur anatomy and locomotion — the four-flipper figure-of-eight swimming motion and its 50% efficiency/thrust gains (Dr Luke Muscutt, University of Southampton)
Biomimicry: how pliosaur flipper vortex mechanics parallel goose-formation aerodynamics and inform dual-rotor wind turbine design
OWI Wind Turbine — James Dyson Award 2018 winner, spherical/tetrahedral design, Venturi-effect wind capture from any direction; Robert builds and tests a 3D-printed version from the US patent
Repurposing waste: abandoned shopping trolleys converted into wheelchairs (Thailand case study); China's surplus bicycle-share fleet as mobility resource
3D scanning with the Creality Ferret Pro: scanning the Gandhi statue in Parliament Square and other public sculptures
Scan the World — MyMiniFactory's open-access 3D scan archive of global cultural artifacts
Art UK — UK's digital catalog of publicly owned sculptures and paintings
Carpon pile — a "forever battery" running since 1950 in Romania; Robert replicates a Type 3 cell from French patent FR 57787, tests temperature dependence of output voltage

Gravity energy storage: Archimedes screw worm gear, spiral-drop ratchet generator, humming-top ratchet mechanism, and a student-built portable gravity-powered emergency generator from the Technological University of the Philippines
materials_mentioned: **Graphite foil** — used as electrodes in the Carpon pile replication
**Sodium carbonate (washing soda)** — electrolyte in the Type 3 Carpon cell

videos.title: 046 VAWTs Inspired By Nature
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
This video explores Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWTs) as an alternative to conventional Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWTs), and investigates how biomimicry — specifically humpback whale tubercles and owl wing trailing-edge serrations — can improve their performance. Robert 3D prints prototype VAWT blades with and without these biological features and measures the RPM difference using a digital tachometer, demonstrating meaningful gains including self-starting capability.

## Key Topics

videos.title: 045 An Unusual Wimshurst Machine
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith builds an unusual Wimshurst electrostatic machine using large circular glass display shelves as the rotating discs instead of the typical plastic. The key innovation is a hubless, axle-free cradle design that uses rim-mounted pulley wheels and motors to drive the discs, allowing the machine to be scaled to virtually any disc size. The completed machine successfully generates high-voltage sparks.

videos.title: 044 The Samara Wind Turbine
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith explores biomimicry in wind turbine design, focusing on the Samara seed (sycamore/maple winged seed) as a blade shape for a small wind turbine. He scans a real sycamore seed, 3D prints it as a three-blade turbine, then refines the blade profile using the free sculpting tool SculptGL. He also investigates whether adding whale-fin-inspired tubercles (nodules along the leading edge) to the blade improves efficiency, drawing on research by marine biologist Frank Fish.

videos.title: 043 Damp Paper - The Future Of Energy
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
This video explores moisture-enabled electricity generation (MEG), where damp cellulose-based paper or graphene oxide films generate electric current through the migration of hydrogen ions across a moisture gradient. Robert demonstrates a basic working experiment using aluminium sheets and paper moistened by breath, then discusses a more advanced graphene oxide-based self-recharging battery developed by Australian company Strategic Elements with CSIRO and the University of New South Wales. The main takeaway is that these devices are surprisingly simple and cheap to fabricate, with ...

Cellulose (paper) as a MEG substrate — dissociation of H⁺ from carboxyl and hydroxyl groups under moisture gradients
Live demonstration: paper + aluminium foil generating ~150 mV from breath
Graphene oxide (GO) as a high-performance MEG material
Strategic Elements / CSIRO / University of New South Wales self-recharging GO battery
Fabrication method for the GO-based MEG device
Target application: electronic skin patches (~$10 billion market)
Scaling potential: wallpaper-scale MEG to power a room from human-generated humidity

videos.title: 042 The Earth Battery
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith explores earth batteries — devices using buried electrodes to generate electricity — covering their history from Galvani and Volta through to modern research. He investigates the central debate over their mechanism (galvanic corrosion, telluric currents, or exoelectrogenic bacteria) through a live outdoor experiment testing various electrode material combinations. A key practical finding is that isolating each cell in a short section of plastic pipe allows cells to be connected in series to increase voltage, something normally prevented by the earth acting as a ...
key_topics: Historical background: Galvani, Volta, the voltaic pile, Lord Kelvin's 1836 seawater battery proposal, and 19th–20th century research into buried electrodes
The generator vs. battery debate: galvanic/corrosion action, telluric earth currents (flowing pole-to-pole and toward the Sun), and exoelectrogenic bacteria as competing or complementary explanations
Effect of electrode orientation (north–south alignment affects current, not voltage), soil moisture, surface area, and electrode material choice
Practical outdoor demonstration: measuring voltage and current from various electrode pairs using a multimeter and dummy load
The series-connection problem: earth-buried cells normally cannot be stacked in series because the soil shorts them together
Plastic pipe isolation technique: driving short ABS pipe sections into the ground before inserting electrodes allows successful series stacking
Exoelectrogenic bacteria: their electrically conductive protein filaments, electron-transport biology (ATP/ADP cycle), and roles as electricity producers, consumers, and biological wires
Potential applications: remote lighting, fuel cells, water purification, NASA interest in off-world energy generation
materials_mentioned: **Carbon rods** — salvaged from zinc-carbon batteries; used as cathode in most pairings; produced the highest voltages
**Zinc** — used as anode in the first test pairing

**Copper** — briefly tested against carbon

**Lead** — tested as anode against brass and carbon; noted as effective but flagged as a safety concern

videos.title: 040 The Power Of Whales - Tubercles On Everything
videos.description: VAWT, HAWT, Water turbines and fans
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
This video explores the aerodynamic benefits of whale tubercles (the bumpy nodules on humpback whale flippers) and applies them to 3D-printed wind turbine and fan blades. Robert builds and compares blades with and without tubercles across several configurations — a sycamore-seed wind turbine, a toroidal fan, and a vertical-axis wind turbine (VAWT) — demonstrating measurable improvements in airflow, efficiency, and noise. He also introduces SculptGL as an accessible 3D sculpting tool for cleaning up scanned blade models.

## Key Topics

videos.title: 039 Charge Controllers
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith demystifies charge controllers by tracing their development from simple electromechanical voltage regulators to modern solid-state electronics, using hands-on demonstrations to show the underlying logic. The core insight is that a charge controller performs a few simple logical jobs — primarily voltage sensing and switching — regardless of whether the implementation is mechanical, electronic, or a pre-built black box. He argues that understanding the *job* a charge controller needs to do is more valuable than searching for the "perfect" implementation.

## Key T...

The role of capacitors in suppressing "hunting" (oscillation instability)
Car alternators as practical examples of built-in charge control
Dump loads as a method for dissipating excess energy
Philosophical digression: "sensing" vs. intrinsic behavior in electrical systems (light switch and Jacquard loom analogies)
Three-relay electromechanical regulator configurations (cutout, voltage, current)
Modern solid-state approach: separating sensing (resistive voltage divider) from switching (transistor)
Arduino-based voltage regulator for controlling an alternator field winding

videos.title: 038 All About Starlite And Invention
videos.description: Archival footage supplied by Internet Archive (at archive.org)
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith explores the history of Starlite — a remarkable heat-resistant coating invented by British hairdresser Maurice Ward in 1986 — examining why it never reached commercialization despite genuine military and NASA interest. He then recreates a Starlite-style intumescent coating using sugar, sodium bicarbonate, borax, sodium silicate, and styrene-butadiene rubber painted onto paper, demonstrating its impressive heat resistance. videos.title: 037 A Flywheel Generator

## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith demonstrates how to build an emergency pumping and power generation system entirely from widely available materials. He constructs ball check valves from plumbing fittings and ping pong balls, assembles accordion (concertina) bellows from venetian blind slats and tape, combines them into a working water pump, and then drives a 3D-printed vane motor to generate electricity. The central takeaway is that this modular system — pump, valves, and generator — can be built cheaply and without power tools from scavenged materials in an emergency.

videos.title: 032 The Secret Of Mathematics
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith argues that mathematics is simply a language — specifically, the language of shape, size, and order — and demystifies the common perception that it is magical or uniquely difficult. He draws parallels between learning maths and learning a foreign language, explaining that the real barrier is social embarrassment rather than inherent complexity. He introduces this as the first in a planned series of mathematics videos.

videos.title: 031 Ancient Mathematics - Where Did Maths Come From
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith traces the origins of mathematics from early nomadic human needs — counting time, animals, and resources — through the development of number systems, the abacus, and measurement, up to the Greeks' pivotal introduction of abstraction and formal proof. He argues that mathematics did not begin as abstract theory but emerged directly from practical survival needs, only becoming abstract when Greek culture demanded logical justification over priestly rules of thumb.

videos.title: 020 Motor Control And Relay Circuits
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith explains relay types and terminology, then demonstrates how to use a double-pole double-throw relay to implement electronic braking for a DC motor. He builds toward a complete motor control circuit — combining braking, an H-bridge, and a latching relay — intended to automate a hole punch machine so it cycles up and down under motor control with automatic stops at each limit.

videos.title: 019 Joule Thieves, MOTs and The Newman Motor
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith demonstrates how to extract coils from microwave oven transformers (MOTs) to build a simple hand-spun generator, then converts the setup into a Newman Motor using an electronic commutation circuit consisting of just a diode and a transistor. The core insight of the video is that this commutation circuit is identical to the Joule Thief / blocking oscillator, revealing the deeper connection between these seemingly separate DIY electronics topics, and showing that a functional brushless DC motor can be built with just three connections and two passive components.

...
key_topics: Extracting and repurposing coils from microwave oven transformers
Thick vs. thin MOT coils: voltage/amperage tradeoffs
Building a simple hand-powered generator from MOT coils and neodymium magnets
History and controversy of the Newman Motor (free energy claims, patent disputes, court cases, Congressional hearing)
Electronic commutation of a DC motor using a diode and transistor (blocking oscillator)
The Joule Thief circuit and its equivalence to the blocking oscillator
The role of trigger coils in self-resonant motor circuits
Winding and forming a serpentine coil (the "Twisted Serpent" printed motor design)

Connection between Bedini's "Simple School Girl" motor and the Joule Thief/Newman circuit

videos.title: 016 The Dumas Effect - A Free Energy Water Heater
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith examines the "Dumas Effect," a claimed super-efficient water heating device invented by Jean-Christophe Dumas, consisting of two electrodes submerged in water and connected to mains AC supply. He traces the same invention through multiple independent discoverers, replicates it using graphite plates and then carbon rods from a zinc-carbon battery, and concludes that the effect is simply ohmic (resistive) heating — not resonance — and is essentially a modern rediscovery of the old "stinger" immersion heater.

## Key Topics
- History of the Dumas Effect and its ind...
key_topics: History of the Dumas Effect and its independent rediscoveries (Dumas, Peter Davey, George Wiseman)
Open-source vs. patent-locked approaches to invention
Commercial development by Heatworks and their "ohmic array" graphite plate heater
Why ohmic/direct water heating is more efficient than conventional resistance-wire immersion heaters (no limescale, no thermal lag)
Debunking the resonance explanation (mains frequency 50–60 Hz vs. water's resonant frequency ~22+ GHz)
Explanation of why AC cannot produce meaningful electrolysis (contrast with DC/HHO cells)
Practical replication using graphite plates and carbon rods from batteries
The "legacy effect" as a barrier to adoption of superior technologies
Electrical safety precautions
materials_mentioned: **Graphite plates** — primary electrode material used by Heatworks and replicated by Robert; inert in water
**Carbon rods (from zinc-carbon batteries)** — used as an alternative electrode material in the second experiment
**Deionised / distilled water** — used as the working fluid in both experiments
**Magnesium oxide** — insulating fill inside conventional resistance-wire heating elements
**Copper** — noted by George Wiseman to contaminate water when used as electrodes
**Hot glue** — used temporarily to space the graphite plates; melted during the experiment, causing a short
**Tile (ceramic)** — drilled and used as a spacer/holder for the carbon rod electrodes

videos.title: 015 A Variety Of Wind Wall Rotors and How To Build Them
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith explores several approaches to building small vertical-axis wind rotors from repurposed and low-cost materials, with the goal of assembling them into a "wind wall" — a bank of rotors that can generate electricity. The video demonstrates a tin-can three-bladed rotor, a community-suggested PVC pipe rotor ("the Conliff rotor"), and a 3D-printed Darrieus-style (Arensky) turbine, all tested against real wind or simulated airflow, with the PVC-pipe version successfully lighting an LED at roughly 25–30 V.

## Key Topics
- History and properties of tin cans as a ubiquit...

videos.title: 014 Inside Things - Fridge Compressor, AC Unit, Lathe, Shredder, Hand Crank Generator
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert tears apart four salvaged machines — a fridge compressor, a hand-crank generator, a portable AC unit, and a broken mini lathe — to reveal their internal workings. The video demystifies everyday engineering by showing how each device is simpler than expected, and highlights how their components can be repurposed, particularly as motors or generators. A recurring theme is that understanding what's inside something unlocks creative reuse.

- Scotch yoke mechanism and induction motor inside the compres...

videos.title: 013 PC Fan Wind Wall
videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert explores whether salvaged PC fans can be converted into wind generators and assembled into a "wind wall" — a flat panel of recycled fans mounted on a plywood board. After converting 100 fans to generators and mounting them with carefully spaced holes, he discovers the plywood board acts as an orifice plate (a simplified Venturi), significantly boosting power output. Outdoor testing in a Canterbury car park suggests the wall performs comparably to a commercial horizontal-axis wind turbine (HAWT) of equivalent swept area.

## Key Topics
- Converting PC fans from motors to gene...

Orifice plates as a cheap alternative to Venturi tubes for accelerating airflow
Bernoulli's principle and its application to ducted/shrouded wind turbines
Wiring 100 generators in parallel columns to combine current output

Robert Murray-Smith builds a "Wind Wall" — a vertical-axis wind turbine array inspired by an online design he later discovered was only a render and had never actually been built. After his initial flat-blade version fails to turn in the wind, he iterates to a Savonius-type rotor design using uPVC guttering, which works successfully and generates electricity via repurposed NEMA stepper motors acting as generators.

Robert Murray-Smith explores the surprisingly broad range of practical applications for mushrooms and fungi beyond their traditional uses in food, medicine, and recreation. ## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith uses the design and build of a folding portable wind turbine as a practical framework for teaching how all electrical power generation systems work. He breaks down any generation system into three universal components — input, link (gearing), and output (generator) — and walks through building each element: a serpentine-coil axial-flux generator, a 3-stage compound planetary gearbox, and folding turbine blades cut from PVC drain pipe. The key takeaway is that "best" design choices are always contextual; understanding the three-component framework lets you reason...
key_topics: The three-component framework for all power generation systems: input, link (gearing), output (generator)
How electric motors and generators are electrically identical
Fleming's right-hand rule explained via a physical vector demonstration with strings
Wire angle relative to magnetic field and its effect on generator output (BLV sinθ formula)
Serpentine coil design vs. standard wound coils — efficiency and ease of construction
Gear theory: transmission, force, and rotational direction; historical context (Antikythera mechanism, Crabble Corn Mill, Dover)
Planetary (epicyclic) gear systems — stackable 3:1 ratio stages yielding 3×, 9×, 27× multiplication
Magnetic gearing as an alternative to mechanical gears
Folding wind turbine blade design using PVC soil/drainage pipe
Bike dynamo integration as a generator option
Rectification strategies: per-coil rectification vs. three-phase rectification; series vs. parallel coil connection

videos.title: 008 A Generator For Every Circumstance

## Overview
Robert builds a large human-powered hamster wheel generator inspired by medieval treadmill cranes and modern "active desk" exercising trends. Using cold molding to construct two large wooden hoops, he assembles the wheel on a rolling sled frame and couples it to a stepper motor via longboard wheels to generate electricity. The finished device produces roughly 50–120 watts — enough to power a games console — demonstrating that human-powered generation is surprisingly practical.

Robert Murray-Smith demonstrates two accessible methods for making graphene at home or in small workshops: laser scribing onto kapton tape or borax-treated paper, and blending graphite with whey protein in water. ## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith, filming during Storm Unice, explores generating electricity directly from falling rain using the triboelectric (static) charge naturally carried by raindrops. He replicates and extends a University of Hong Kong study showing that a simple capacitor made from aluminium foil and PTFE tape can produce surprisingly large voltages when water rolls across it, ultimately building a rotating wheel version capable of harvesting energy from flowing water.

videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert demonstrates how to build a hand-powered emergency phone charger using a treadle-crank mechanism, a 3D-printed gear train, a magnet disc, and a serpentine coil generator. The device converts arm or leg motion into electricity via a 9:1 gear ratio, passes the AC output through a rectifier and voltage regulator, and feeds a USB port — successfully charging a phone during testing.

## Key Topics
- History and mechanical efficiency of crank vs. treadle mechanisms
- 3D-printed generator design using TinkerCAD (disc with magnets, gear chain, coil cradle)
- Gear ratios and compound...
key_topics: History and mechanical efficiency of crank vs. treadle mechanisms
3D-printed generator design using TinkerCAD (disc with magnets, gear chain, coil cradle)
Gear ratios and compound gear chains (3:1 × 3:1 = 9:1)
AC generation and the need for rectification
DC voltage stabilisation using a cheap LM2596S-based buck regulator board
Serpentine coil design and Fleming's right-hand generator rule
Edge-generation principle (magnets placed at the disc rim for higher tip speed)
Human power output estimates (~60 W per arm, ~120 W legs, ~500 W full cross-ski body motion)
Compact redesign with offset gear chain and single rocker arm
materials_mentioned: **Neodymium magnets** — 1 cm × 3 mm circular disc magnets (first design) and 1 cm × 2 mm disc magnets (second design), polarised north–south alternately on the rotor disc
**6 mm steel bar** — used as axles throughout
**12 mm × 6 mm × 4 mm bearings** — standard bearing size used at all shaft locations
**4 mm bar** — used in the compact redesign's crank pivot
**Super glue** — used to fix cranks to the 6 mm bar
**Enamelled copper wire (hair-thin)** — wound into the serpentine coil
**LM2596S buck converter module** — voltage regulator chip, accepts 3–40 V in, outputs 1.5–35 V, rated 3 A

videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith dismantles an expired CO2 fire extinguisher to explore its reusable components, primarily the thick aluminum tube that forms the body. He demonstrates three distinct projects using salvaged parts: building a resonant bell-speaker using a piezo element, turning the aluminum ring into DIY headphones, and constructing a simple beta/gamma-type Stirling engine using the tube as the cylinder.

## Key Topics
- Disassembly and internal anatomy of a CO2 fire extinguisher
- Repurposing the aluminum tube body as a resonant bell/speaker
- Making headphones and a flat-panel ...
key_topics: Disassembly and internal anatomy of a CO2 fire extinguisher
Repurposing the aluminum tube body as a resonant bell/speaker
Making headphones and a flat-panel ceiling speaker from piezo buzzers
Removing the fire extinguisher's resin paint coating
Making a decorative column vase from the cleaned aluminum tube
Building a low-temperature-differential Stirling engine using the tube as a cylinder
materials_mentioned: **Aluminum** — the main body tube of the CO2 extinguisher; noted as surprisingly thick-walled
**Brass** — the valve assembly inside the extinguisher
**CO2 (carbon dioxide)** — the pressurized gas stored inside
**Piezo element (buzzer)** — sourced from gift cards or circuit boards; used as a speaker driver
**Latex rubber / exercise resistance bands** — used as the working diaphragm and displacer membrane for the Stirling engine
**750 ml paint can** — used as the displacer piston inside the Stirling engine cylinder
**Resin coating** — the red paint on the extinguisher body, noted as difficult to remove
**Magnets and coil** — mentioned speculatively for electricity generation from the Stirling engine output

videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith presents a comprehensive omnibus video covering the complete build of a dual rotor wind turbine, consolidating several previous videos with added detail from viewer questions. The turbine uses 3D-printed blades hardened with superglue and a custom axial flux generator, achieving ~1500 RPM and ~30V/100mA output in winds of just 2.5–3.4 m/s — a result Robert attributes to the dual-rotor configuration exceeding the Betz limit constraints of single-rotor designs.

## Key Topics
- Dual rotor wind turbine concept and its alignment with current academic research
- 3D-p...
key_topics: Dual rotor wind turbine concept and its alignment with current academic research
3D-printed blade design and structural reinforcement using superglue
Blade orientation, arrangement, and 60-degree offset between rotors
Radial flux vs. axial flux motor/generator comparison
Design and winding of a serpentine coil for the axial flux generator
Betz limit and how dual rotors and ducted designs can approach or appear to exceed it
Field testing on a hilltop with LED load as output indicator
materials_mentioned: **Thin/precision superglue (cyanoacrylate)** — used to coat and strengthen 3D-printed PLA blades along their print-layer grain
**Neodymium magnets** (15mm × 1cm, 32 total) — arranged north-south alternating in the axial flux generator discs
**Hair-thin copper wire** — wound into serpentine coil on a wooden former
**M8 bolt and washer** — used as the axle and spacer between magnet disc and coil disc
**Nitrile gloves** — recommended for handling superglue

videos.summary_excerpt:
## Overview
Robert Murray-Smith traces the centuries-long history of solar thermal energy, arguing it has repeatedly been adopted during fuel crises only to be abandoned when easier options returned. Using this historical context as motivation, he demonstrates how to build practical solar thermal devices — a solar kettle and a solar barbecue — from cheap evacuated tubes and 3D-printed parts, and explores concentrated solar power techniques including parabolic reflectors and a DIY Fresnel lens made from water-filled plastic tubing.

## Key Topics
- Historical cycles of solar thermal adoption fr...
key_topics: Historical cycles of solar thermal adoption from ancient times through the Industrial Revolution to the present
Why solar thermal is repeatedly overlooked in favour of solar PV despite its maturity and efficiency
Evacuated solar thermal tubes: how they work, their efficiency (94–96%), and cost (~£10)
Heat pipe principles and construction
Building a 3D-printed solar kettle using an evacuated tube and a parabolic reflector
Building a 3D-printed solar barbecue (same concept oriented horizontally)
Large-scale concentrated solar power: heliostats, parabolic troughs, and solar furnaces
Rice University nanoparticle steam generation research
Stirling engines as an alternative to steam for solar thermal power
Red River College flat-panel Stirling engine design
Parabolic reflector construction methods (mylar vacuum forming, petal/flower fold-up, repurposed satellite dishes, woks, fire pits)
Reflective materials for parabolic surfaces
Fresnel lenses: history, principles, and a DIY version made from coiled polypropylene tube filled with water
materials_mentioned: **Evacuated solar thermal glass tubes** — vacuum-insulated tubes with a selective black coating for heat absorption
**Barium (and alternatives: aluminium, sodium, potassium)** — used as the getter inside the vacuum tube to absorb residual oxygen
**Copper pipe** — used for the heat pipe construction
**Acetone (and isopropanol/alcohol)** — working fluid inside DIY heat pipes; evaporates at low temperature
**Aluminium** — fin material inside tubes; also used as reflective sheeting (sticky-back HVAC tape, foil)
**Transition metals (tungsten, cobalt, nickel) in a ceramic matrix** — the magnetron-sputtered selective absorber coating on evacuated tubes
**Cadmium selenide** — mentioned in the history of the photovoltaic effect (1932)
**Silicon** — first silicon solar cells (1954)
**Metal and carbon nanoparticles (including graphene)** — Rice University research; absorb sunlight and transfer heat to water to generate steam at sub-zero ambient temperatures
**Mylar sheet** — vacuum/pressure-formed parabolic reflectors
**Polypropylene clear tube (5 mm, 7 m)** — coiled and water-filled to act as a DIY Fresnel lens
**Rust-Oleum Mirror Effect spray paint** — used to create mirror finish on reflector guttering
**Aluminium sticky-back tape (HVAC type)** — reflective surface for parabolic reflectors
**Decorative metal foil (self-adhesive)** — alternative reflective material
**Kitchen foil (extra strong)** — used with spray adhesive as a reflective surface
**Stainless steel sheet** — bent into a food shelf for the solar barbecue
**110 mm PVC guttering** — used as the curved reflector wings on the solar kettle/barbecue
